topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a familiar, unwelcome intrusion. A profile picture loaded: a grainy, slightly mocking selfie from a decade ago. My stomach tightened, a cold, heavy knot forming just beneath my ribs. It was him. Mark. The phantom of scraped knees and whispered taunts in the cafeteria. I didn't even bother clicking to view the profile fully. I just stared at the little blue bubble, the cursor blinking mockingly. A slow, deliberate breath escaped my lungs, and I let the request sit there, unacknowledged, for a full minute before finally tapping 'Ignore.' The relief that washed over me was thin, like watered-down tea, quickly replaced by the dull ache of knowing this was just another predictable ripple in the stagnant pool of my life.

[story 2]

I saw the name pop up, and a distinct flatness settled over my features. It wasn't anger, or even surprise; just a profound lack of energy. I leaned back in my worn armchair, the springs groaning in protest beneath my weight. *Mark.* The thought surfaced, unbidden, and I watched it drift past, uninteresting. I didn't feel the urge to confront him, or even to block the request. Instead, I picked up the remote and turned on a muted documentary. The sound of the narrator’s voice was flat, monotonous, mirroring the feeling inside me. I simply waited for the notification to fade into the background noise of the day, a piece of digital dust I would eventually sweep away without a second thought.

[story 3]

My fingers hovered over the screen for a long moment. The image of Mark—that sneer etched into his face—flashed behind my eyes. There was no spark of indignation, no sudden surge of defensive energy. Just a quiet acknowledgment of the pattern. I sighed, a sound so faint it might have been the settling of the old house. I didn't open the message window. Why engage? It was like watching a tide come in; you just wait for it to recede. I closed the app, the screen going dark, and turned my attention back to the book resting on my lap, the words suddenly seeming much more compelling than any digital interaction.
